era_context: The mid-8th century CE spans the height of the Abbasid Caliphate's consolidation of power in the Islamic world, the Tang Dynasty's dominance in East Asia, and the Carolingian Renaissance in Western Europe. This period marks major geopolitical realignments: the Abbasids have recently overthrown the Umayyads (750 CE), the An Lushan Rebellion is destabilizing Tang China, and the Frankish kingdom under Pepin III is establishing itself as a major European power. Byzantine Empire faces external pressures from Arab and Bulgar forces.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Frankish realm (sparse documentation)
Southeast Asia (limited contemporary records in European/Islamic sources)

events.title: An Lushan Rebellion begins
events.region: Tang Dynasty, China (Hebei and Henan provinces)
events.description: An Lushan, a Sogdian-Turkic general in the Tang military, declares himself emperor and launches a major rebellion against the Tang Dynasty. This marks the beginning of one of the most destructive civil wars in Chinese history, lasting until 763 CE. The rebellion will kill millions, destabilize the Tang state, and fundamentally weaken central authority, leading to the rise of regional military governors (jiedushi) and the eventual fragmentation of Tang power. The event reshapes East Asian geopolitics for generations.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE
Contemporary Tang administrative records and memorials

events.certainty_note: The rebellion's outbreak in 755 CE is firmly established in multiple Tang historical records and later compilations. Dating is precise.
cross_references: Follows from 750-001 (Abbasid overthrow of Umayyads, contemporary regional instability)

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate consolidates control in Iraq and Persia
events.description: Five years after overthrowing the Umayyad Caliphate (750 CE), the Abbasid dynasty under Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775) continues consolidating administrative and military control over the Islamic heartland. This period sees the establishment of a centralized bureaucracy, the recruitment of Persian administrative talent, and the suppression of remaining Umayyad loyalists and other rival factions. The Abbasids are laying the groundwork for the construction of Baghdad (founded 762 CE) as their new capital.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The Abbasid consolidation is well-documented in Islamic historiography and numismatic evidence. The year 755 CE marks al-Mansur's accession and the execution of Abu Muslim, a pivotal moment.
cross_references: Part of broader Abbasid rise (750–800 CE)

events.title: Pepin III's Frankish kingdom expands in Italy
events.description: Pepin III (the Short), king of the Franks, campaigns in Italy to support Pope Stephen II against Lombard encroachment. These campaigns, which began in 754 CE, continue through 755 and result in Frankish military victories that secure Papal territorial interests. The Frankish intervention establishes a crucial alliance between the Frankish monarchy and the Papacy, laying the foundation for the later Carolingian Empire and the concept of Papal temporal power backed by Frankish military might.

sources.name: Liber Pontificalis (Book of the Popes), compiled 8th–9th centuries
Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom)
Paul the Deacon, History of the Lombards (Historia Langobardorum), compiled late 8th century

events.certainty_note: The campaigns are attested in Frankish and Papal sources, though exact chronology of individual years (754–756) shows minor variation across sources. 755 CE is a probable date for continued operations.
cross_references: Leads to 756-001 (Donation of Pepin, territorial settlement)

events.title: Byzantine-Bulgarian tensions and frontier warfare
events.description: The Byzantine Empire under Constantine V continues military operations against the First Bulgarian Empire under Khan Krum's predecessors. Frontier raids and skirmishes characterize this period, as both powers contest control of the Balkans. These conflicts are part of a longer pattern of Byzantine-Bulgarian rivalry that will intensify in the 9th century. The exact events of 755 CE are poorly documented, but the broader military context is clear from later chronicles.
key_figures: Bulgarian khans (identity uncertain for this specific year)

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle (Chronographia), compiled early 9th century

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Byzantine-Bulgarian conflict is confirmed, but specific events in 755 CE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the era context rather than a precisely documented event.
cross_references: Part of longer Byzantine-Bulgarian conflict (7th–11th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the An Lushan Rebellion's outbreak in 755 CE is universally accepted, but modern scholarship has questioned earlier narratives that portrayed An Lushan as a purely external threat. Recent work emphasizes that An Lushan was deeply embedded in Tang military and political structures, and the rebellion reflected internal Tang factional conflicts as much as external invasion. Additionally, some scholars debate whether the rebellion's death toll (traditionally cited as 10–15 million) is exaggerated in later sources. The Frankish-Papal alliance is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a deliberate 'donation' when it was more pragmatic military cooperation; the formal 'Donation of Pepin' is a later construct.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 755 CE is heavily skewed toward East Asia (Tang China) and the Islamic world, where contemporary administrative records and near-contemporary chronicles survive. Western European sources are sparse and often compiled centuries later. Byzantine sources are fragmentary. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from the historical record for this year. The surviving sources are also shaped by court-sponsored historiography (Tang, Abbasid, Frankish) and religious institutions (Papal, Byzantine), which may emphasize political legitimacy and downplay internal instability or military failures.

graph_edges.note: An Lushan Rebellion's suppression in 763 CE; the rebellion lasted 8 years
Long-term fragmentation of Tang authority and rise of regional military governors
Abbasid consolidation enabled the founding of Baghdad as the new capital
Frankish military success led to the Donation of Pepin and Papal territorial establishment
An Lushan Rebellion and Abbasid consolidation occurred simultaneously, reshaping Eurasian geopolitics
